---
title: "Portfolio-Final-The-Simpsons"
format: html
author: "Trevor Edgull"
execute:
  echo: false
embed-resources: true
---


## Data Description

My dataset is about the TV show The Simpsons, which included information from seasons 1-28. The dataset is from a tidytuesday submission, where the data was extracted from imdb using Kaggle (https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-02-04/readme.md). While the tidytuesday submission had 4 csv files included, the dataset I used was the the simpson_characters.csv, which included information for every episode aired from Season 1 through Season 28. For each episode, the dataset records the season number, the episode's position within the season, the original air date, the U.S. viewership in millions, and the IMDb audience rating. In total there are 600 episodes represented. 

## Data Cleaning

Before making any visualizations, I made a few small changes to the dataset so it would be easier to work with. First, I narrowed it down to just the columns I needed for this project: the season, episode number, title, air date, U.S. viewership, and IMDb rating. The original file contained other columns like image links and video URLs, which I did not need.
Next, I noticed that a handful of episodes were missing either their viewership numbers or their IMDb ratings. Six episodes had no viewership recorded, and three were missing ratings. For each visualization below, I left out only the episodes that were missing the specific number being shown, so that no chart is built on incomplete data. The rest of the dataset was already clean and consistent, so no other adjustments were necessary.

```{r}
#| label: set-up
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
```



```{r}
#| label: cleaning-data
#| echo: false

simpsons_episodes <- read.csv("simpsons-dataset/simpsons_episodes.csv")

simpsons_episodes_clean <- simpsons_episodes |> #cleaning dataset to select only needed columns
  select(season, number_in_season, number_in_series, title,
         original_air_date, us_viewers_in_millions, imdb_rating) |>
  mutate(original_air_date = as.Date(original_air_date)) |>
  filter(!is.na(us_viewers_in_millions)) #removing missing values

season_avg <- simpsons_episodes_clean |> #creating new column for average viewship of each season
  group_by(season) |>
  summarise(
    avg_viewers = mean(us_viewers_in_millions),
    n_episodes  = n(),
    .groups     = "drop")

simpsons_ratings <- simpsons_episodes |>
  select(season, number_in_season, title,
         original_air_date, imdb_rating, imdb_votes) |>
  mutate(original_air_date = as.Date(original_air_date)) |>
  filter(!is.na(imdb_rating))  

season_rating <- simpsons_ratings |>
  group_by(season) |>
  summarise(
    avg_rating = mean(imdb_rating),
    n_episodes = n(),
    .groups    = "drop"
  )
```
## Average Season Viewership

The first visualization shows the average number of U.S. viewers (in millions) for each season of The Simpsons, from Season 1 through Season 28. Each bar represents one season, and the height of the bar tells you how many people, on average, tuned in to watch a typical episode that year. This view makes it easy to see how the show's audience changed over time. There is a pattern that shows a strong average viewship base in the early seasons of The Simpsons with a decline in viewership as seasons progress. There is some resurgance in viewership numbers from season 12 to season 13, however the decline in viewship still continues. 

```{r}
#| label: Avg-Season-Viewership
#| fig-cap: "The barplot shows each season of The Simpsons on the x-axis. The y-axis represents the average number of viewership per episode of each season of The Simpsons. Above each bar shows the exact number of average viewership of each season."

ggplot(season_avg, aes(x = season, y = avg_viewers)) + #creating barplot for average viewership per season
  geom_col(fill = "yellow", color = "blue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_viewers, 1)),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:28) +
  labs(
    title    = "Average U.S. Viewership per Simpsons Season",
    x        = "Season",
    y        = "Average viewers (millions)",
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


```
## Average Season IMDB Rating

This visualization shows the average IMDb audience rating for each season of The Simpsons, from Season 1 through Season 28. IMDb ratings are scored on a scale from 1 to 10, where higher scores mean audiences thought the episodes were better. Each point on the line represents one season's average rating, and the connecting line makes it easy to see how audience reception changed over time. The chart reveals that ratings climbed quickly during the early seasons and reached their peak in the middle seasons, before gradually lowering in later years. Even at their lowest, however, the average ratings never fell sharply.

```{r}
#| label: Avg-IMDB-rating
#| fig-cap: "A line chart with points showing the average IMDb audience rating for each season of The Simpsons from Season 1 to Season 28. The line begins around 7.5 in Season 1, climbs sharply to a peak above 8.0 in the middle seasons (roughly Seasons 4 through 8), then slopes downward gradually through the later seasons, settling into the low 7s by Season 28. The overall shape resembles a gentle hill, with no sudden drops."

ggplot(season_rating, aes(x = season, y = avg_rating)) + #creaing line plot of average IMDB ranking for each season of The Simpsons
  geom_line(color = "pink", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 28, by = 1)) + #labeling x-axis for each season
  labs(title = "Simpsons IMDb Ratings by Season",
       x = "Season", y = "Average IMDb rating") +
  theme_minimal()
```
